topic: Pine Mountain Fire (USFS prescribed burn that escaped 2026-05-07) intersected with Bend's Section R-327 wildfire mitigation building code (effective 2026-05-15)
data_accuracy_gate: All on-screen and spoken figures trace to a primary or near-primary source verified within 2026-05-10. No memory-recalled or estimated figures.

figures.claim: Pine Mountain Fire is approximately 14 miles southeast of Bend
figures.verbatim_quote: The fire on Pine Mountain, about 14 miles southeast of Bend, grew to 2,866 acres by Friday afternoon

figures.claim: Pine Mountain Fire current size as of 2026-05-10 9:20 AM
figures.verbatim_quote: The Pine Mountain Fire is at 2,589 acres with a decrease in acreage due to mapping refinement following an infrared flight. The fire is 70% contained.

figures.claim: Fire started when a USFS prescribed burn jumped containment lines on Thursday May 7, 2026
figures.verbatim_quote: On Thursday morning, the Forest Service announced it was canceling the burn due to unfavorable conditions. The agency declared the burn had turned into a wildfire that afternoon.

figures.claim: USFS spokesperson said unexpected conditions caused the prescribed burn to escape
figures.source: OPB (quoting Jaimie Olle, Central Oregon Fire Management Service)
figures.verbatim_quote: "We experienced conditions that were unexpected," said Jaimie Olle, Central Oregon Fire Management Service spokesperson.

figures.claim: Fire is 70% contained as of 2026-05-10 9:20 AM
appears_in: b4_contained overlay ("70% CONTAINED · AS OF MAY 10 9:20 AM")

figures.claim: Section R-327 wildfire mitigation building code takes effect May 15, 2026 for new residential building permits
figures.verbatim_quote: The new requirements will take effect May 15, 2026, and apply to new residential building permit applications submitted on or after that date.

figures.claim: Section R327 of the Oregon Residential Specialty Code is what Bend adopted
figures.verbatim_quote: The Bend City Council has adopted Section R327 of the Oregon Residential Specialty Code, establishing home-hardening requirements designed to improve wildfire resilience for new, detached residential construction in Bend.

figures.figure: Ignition-resistant siding, hardened roofing, sealed vents
figures.claim: R327 mandates noncombustible/ignition-resistant materials for roofing, gutters, windows, vents, and siding
figures.verbatim_quote: The new requirements include the use of noncombustible or ignition resistant materials for exterior components such as roofing, gutters, windows, vents and siding.

figures.figure: Sisters and unincorporated Deschutes County already have R-327
figures.claim: Sisters and unincorporated Deschutes County had already adopted R-327 prior to Bend's adoption
appears_in: b8_sisters overlay ("SISTERS ✓ DESCHUTES CO ✓ BEND → MAY 15")
figures.verbatim_quote: The code is already in effect for the City of Sisters and unincorporated areas of Deschutes County, supporting a consistent regional approach to community wildfire resilience.

figures.figure: Only new permits / does not apply to existing homes
figures.claim: R327 applies only to new residential building permit applications, not to additions or renovations of existing buildings
figures.verbatim_quote: It does not apply to additions or renovations of existing buildings.

non_figure_assets.provenance: Bend City Council public meeting recordings (Bend Granicus archive — public record). Used here as visually-appropriate b-roll for the policy-pivot beats (B5, B6). The clips do NOT depict the R-327 vote specifically — they are generic council-chamber footage from January 7 and February 4, 2026 work sessions. Used for visual symbolism only.
